children.name: Atelocynus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the short-eared dog, a rare and secretive canid of Amazonian rainforests, distinguished by its unusually short ears and dark colouration, seldom observed in the wild.

children.name: Canis
children.description: The wolves, jackals, and domestic dog — the most ecologically diverse canid genus — spanning from the great grey wolf and its domesticated descendant to the coyote and golden jackal across Eurasia, Africa, and the Americas.

children.name: Cerdocyon
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the crab-eating fox, a widespread and adaptable South American canid of forests, savannas, and grasslands, omnivorous despite its misleading name.

children.name: Chrysocyon
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the maned wolf, the tallest wild canid, with long rusty-red legs adapted for seeing over tall savanna grass in South America's Cerrado and Pantanal regions.

children.name: Cuon
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the dhole, a highly social pack-hunting wild dog of South and Southeast Asia whose whistling calls and cooperative hunts allow packs to take prey much larger than themselves.

children.name: Dusicyon
children.description: A genus containing the Falkland Islands wolf, the only land mammal native to the Falkland Islands, which went extinct in 1876 — one of the few documented historical extinction events for a canid.

children.name: Lupulella
children.description: The African jackals — the side-striped and black-backed jackal — recently separated from Canis, inhabiting sub-Saharan Africa as versatile omnivores and important scavengers in savanna ecosystems.

children.name: Lycalopex
children.description: The South American foxes, six species of fox-like canids distributed across Patagonia, the Andes, and the pampas, filling the ecological niche of small opportunistic carnivores across diverse South American habitats.

children.name: Lycaon
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the African wild dog, a highly endangered pack hunter of sub-Saharan Africa with a mottled tricoloured coat, renowned for its cooperative hunts and unusually high success rate.

children.name: Nyctereutes
children.description: A small genus containing the raccoon dog(s), fox-sized East Asian canids with raccoon-like facial markings that are unique among canids in their ability to enter torpor during harsh winters.

children.name: Otocyon
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the bat-eared fox, an African fox with disproportionately large ears used to detect harvester termites and beetle larvae, which form the bulk of its highly specialised diet.

children.name: Speothos
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the bush dog, a small, semi-aquatic pack hunter of Central and South American forests, with webbed feet and a stocky body well adapted to pursuing prey into water.

children.name: Urocyon
children.description: The grey foxes, two species that are the only North American canids able to climb trees, using rotating wrist joints to ascend oaks and escape predators in brushy habitats from Canada to Venezuela.

children.name: Vulpes
children.description: The true foxes, around 12 species including the ubiquitous red fox — the most widely distributed wild carnivore on Earth — characterised by their pointed muzzles, bushy tails, and adaptability to habitats from Arctic to desert.
